network.overall_strategy: This network targets proline through a chemically conservative prebiotic family centered on glutamate-semialdehyde / 1-pyrroline-5-carboxylate (P5C), while avoiding poorly supported shortcuts criticized in prior attempts. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies robust C1-C3 feedstocks (formate, acetate, pyruvate, alanine) from CO2/H2 on iron-sulfur minerals. Surface chemistry supplies HCN, formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, oxaloacetate, aspartate, and glutamate via cyanosulfidic photochemistry, borate-stabilized sugar chemistry, and Strecker/transamination-like sequences under wet-dry cycling. Biochemical/prebiotic closure then converts glutamate to glutamate semialdehyde, cyclizes to P5C, and reduces to proline; stereochemical enrichment to L-proline is not assumed at the synthetic step itself but is attributed to downstream mineral or chiral-phase selection. The network is organized around hubs HCN, pyruvate, oxaloacetate, glutamate, and P5C, with multiple convergent routes from hydrothermal and surface environments into the proline family.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface hydrogenation of CO2 to surface-bound formyl/formate equivalents followed by release as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Reduction of CO2 to formate on iron-sulfur minerals under alkaline hydrothermal conditions is among the best-supported vent reactions and provides a defensible C1 entry point for the network (He et al., 2010; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reduction of CO2/formate to acetate
reactions.mechanism: Sequential C1 reduction and C-C coupling on Fe-S/oxide surfaces produces acetyl-like species hydrolyzing to acetate.
reactions.reasoning: Acetate formation from CO2/H2 in hydrothermal systems is consistent with acetyl-CoA-analog vent chemistry and Fe-mineral-catalyzed carbon fixation (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from CO2-derived carbon

conditions.other: anoxic; vent pH gradients; reductive carboxylation-like mineral chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Acetyl-like carbon is further carboxylated/oxidized on mixed Fe oxide-sulfide surfaces to generate alpha-keto acid character, yielding pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate generation in abiotic hydrothermal simulations is less secure than formate/acetate but has experimental and systems-level support in Fe-catalyzed CO2 fixation networks; retained here as a moderate-confidence hydrothermal feeder rather than the sole route (Cody et al., 2000; Varma et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine
reactions.mechanism: Imine formation from pyruvate and ammonia followed by mineral-assisted reduction yields alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids on mineral surfaces is a plausible vent process and alanine provides a realistic amino donor for later transamination chemistry (Barge et al., 2019; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Surface UV-cyanosulfidic generation of HCN from simple C/N feedstocks

conditions.other: UV irradiation; anoxic shallow pools; photoredox sulfur chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical redox cycling in sulfur-bearing surface settings generates reduced nitrile carbon, furnishing HCN as a cyanosulfidic reservoir species.
reactions.reasoning: Rather than deriving HCN from formaldehyde plus ammonia, this network places HCN in a more defensible cyanosulfidic photochemical context consistent with surface prebiotic chemistry (Patel et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Surface hydrolysis of HCN to formamide
reactions.mechanism: Partial hydration of nitrile carbon converts HCN into amide carbonyl functionality, yielding formamide.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide is a recognized prebiotic reservoir deriving from cyanide hydrolysis and links nitrile chemistry to broader surface photochemistry (Saladino et al., 2012; Ferus et al., 2017).

reactions.name: Surface photochemical conversion of formamide to formaldehyde reservoir
reactions.mechanism: Photochemical fragmentation and hydrolysis of C1 amide reservoirs furnish reactive carbonyl species including formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This step is included as a conservative C1 carbonyl source from surface nitrogen-carbon reservoirs; while indirect and context-dependent, formamide photochemistry is experimentally linked to diverse reduced carbon products. This step is somewhat speculative but more defensible than the previously criticized direct formate-to-formaldehyde hydrothermal step (Saladino et al., 2012; Ferus et al., 2017).

reactions.name: Surface formose entry to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: evaporitic concentration; borate stabilization of sugars

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde undergoes base/mineral-mediated C-C coupling to the C2 sugar aldehyde, with borate suppressing destructive side reactions.
reactions.reasoning: Glycolaldehyde is a central prebiotic sugar precursor accessible from formaldehyde chemistry, especially in borate-containing settings that stabilize carbohydrate intermediates (Ricardo et al., 2004; Ritson & Sutherland, 2012).

reactions.name: Surface aldol extension of glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Aldol addition between C1 and C2 aldehydes generates the C3 triose glyceraldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Aldol growth from glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde is a standard element of prebiotic sugar chemistry and provides a plausible C3 carbonyl route into central metabolites (Ricardo et al., 2004; Ritson & Sutherland, 2012).

reactions.name: Surface oxidative assembly of pyruvate from glyceraldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Photochemical oxidation/rearrangement of triose carbonyls under phosphate-rich conditions yields alpha-keto acid functionality, producing pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: A surface route into pyruvate is required to avoid overreliance on hydrothermal pyruvate alone. Pyruvate and related alpha-keto acids arise in cyanosulfidic and photochemical carbonyl networks, though exact route details remain system-dependent; this step is included as a moderate-confidence surface feeder (Patel et al., 2015; Muchowska et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Surface cyanohydrin-based assembly of oxaloacetate from pyruvate and HCN
reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate forms a cyanohydrin that, upon hydrolysis and partial oxidation-state adjustment, furnishes the C4 alpha-keto dicarboxylate skeleton of oxaloacetate.
reactions.reasoning: This route replaces earlier unsupported direct carboxylation schemes with a nitrile-mediated chain-extension logic that is more aligned with prebiotic cyanide chemistry. While not fully settled experimentally for oxaloacetate specifically, cyanohydrin homologation is chemically more credible than the previously criticized pyruvate-to-oxaloacetate shortcut (Eschenmoser, 2007; Muchowska et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Surface transamination/reductive amination of oxaloacetate to aspartate
reactions.mechanism: Aspartate forms by amino transfer or net reductive amination of oxaloacetate, with alanine/pyruvate serving as a plausible amino transfer pair.
reactions.reasoning: Transamination among alpha-keto acids and amino acids is a chemically grounded way to populate amino-acid networks from hydrothermal alanine and surface ketoacids, and is more defensible than earlier ad hoc chain-extension steps (Muchowska et al., 2019; Powner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Biochemical homologation of aspartate/oxaloacetate to 2-oxoglutarate

conditions.other: anoxic pool-interface chemistry; iterative carbonyl/carboxyl exchange

reactions.mechanism: Network-level C1/C4 coupling and oxidative deamination logic converts aspartate/oxaloacetate-derived carbon skeletons into the C5 alpha-keto acid 2-oxoglutarate.
reactions.reasoning: Direct prebiotic synthesis of 2-oxoglutarate remains an open problem; this step is explicitly acknowledged as a gap-bridging, moderate-speculation network connection needed to join robust C4 chemistry to the glutamate/proline family. Its inclusion is justified by abiotic protometabolic studies that generate multiple TCA-like intermediates under Fe-mediated conditions, though not always as a single clean transformation (Muchowska et al., 2017; Keller et al., 2014).

reactions.name: Biochemical reductive amination of 2-oxoglutarate to glutamate
reactions.mechanism: Imine formation at the alpha-keto position followed by reduction yields glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Among late-stage steps, reductive amination of alpha-keto acids is chemically plausible and provides the correct biochemical family precursor for proline. This is substantially more realistic than converting pyruvate directly into higher amino acids (Barge et al., 2019; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Biochemical dehydration/cyclization of glutamate to pyroglutamate
reactions.mechanism: Intramolecular condensation of the gamma-carboxyl with the amino group forms the five-membered lactam pyroglutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyroglutamate is a credible dehydration product of glutamate under prebiotic drying conditions and serves as a realistic branch point within glutamate-family chemistry (Forsythe et al., 2015; Rodriguez-Garcia et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Biochemical partial reduction of glutamate to glutamate semialdehyde

conditions.other: anoxic; concentration in pools; activation chemistry required

reactions.mechanism: Selective activation of one carboxylate followed by reduction furnishes the corresponding semialdehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Selective conversion of glutamate to glutamate semialdehyde is a necessary but challenging prebiotic step. It is retained because the glutamate semialdehyde/P5C/proline manifold is chemically correct for proline family assembly; however, the exact prebiotic activation chemistry remains unresolved and this step should be viewed as constrained speculation rather than an experimentally established one-pot transformation (Keller et al., 2014; Muchowska et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Biochemical interconversion of pyroglutamate and P5C
reactions.mechanism: Reductive ring-carbonyl adjustment or hydrolytic ring-state exchange moves the glutamate family between lactam and imine/carboxylate forms, yielding P5C.
reactions.reasoning: Pyroglutamate is not assumed to be the dominant route, but it is a chemically meaningful side branch converging on the same cyclic imine family as glutamate semialdehyde. This branch is moderately speculative yet more chemically interpretable than the previously criticized pyroglutamate shortcut without family context (Forsythe et al., 2015; Muchowska et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Biochemical spontaneous cyclization of glutamate semialdehyde to P5C
reactions.mechanism: The gamma-semialdehyde condenses intramolecularly with the amino group to form the cyclic imine P5C.
reactions.reasoning: Once glutamate semialdehyde exists, cyclization to P5C is straightforward and chemically well grounded; this is one of the strongest late-stage steps in the target manifold (Nelson & Cox, biochemistry framework; adapted to prebiotic concentrated-solution conditions).

reactions.name: Biochemical reduction of P5C to proline
reactions.mechanism: Reduction of the cyclic imine in P5C gives pyrrolidine-2-carboxylate, i.e. proline.
reactions.reasoning: Reduction of a cyclic imine to the corresponding saturated amine is chemically reasonable and provides the final closure to proline. The product is shown as L-proline as target nomenclature, but this step itself is not assumed to generate high enantiomeric excess; any L-enrichment would require secondary selection processes (Blackmond, 2010; Bonner, 1991).

pathways.name: Hydrothermal acetate-pyruvate-glutamate semialdehyde route
pathways.description: A vent-first route from CO2 reduction through acetate and hydrothermal pyruvate, then into oxaloacetate, glutamate, P5C, and proline.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal alanine-assisted aspartate route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate is aminated to alanine, which later serves as amino donor to oxaloacetate on the surface before entry into the glutamate/P5C manifold.

pathways.name: Surface HCN-formamide-formose to pyruvate route
pathways.description: A surface-dominated route from HCN through formamide, formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, and pyruvate before joining the common proline family branch.

pathways.name: Surface glycolaldehyde-centered route
pathways.description: A shorter surface route using glycolaldehyde as the main carbon hub into glyceraldehyde, pyruvate, and the glutamate branch.

pathways.name: Mixed hydrothermal-surface pyruvate convergence route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate and surface HCN converge directly at oxaloacetate via cyanohydrin homologation, then proceed through glutamate semialdehyde.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formate plus surface C4 assembly route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formate is carried into surface and biochemical chemistry as the C1 contributor to the C4-to-C5 expansion that gives 2-oxoglutarate and then proline.

pathways.name: Pyroglutamate branch from glutamate
pathways.description: After glutamate formation, wet-dry cycling diverts material through pyroglutamate before convergence on P5C and proline.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal glutamate-to-pyroglutamate route
pathways.description: Vent-fixed carbon flows through hydrothermal pyruvate into the glutamate family, then uses the pyroglutamate side branch rather than semialdehyde-first closure.

pathways.name: Alanine-mediated mixed-environment route with semialdehyde closure
pathways.description: Hydrothermal alanine is explicitly used as amino-transfer currency to populate aspartate and ultimately glutamate, followed by semialdehyde cyclization.

pathways.name: Maximum-convergence network route
pathways.description: A redundancy-rich path that samples both surface carbonyl buildup and hydrothermal fixed-carbon inputs before entering the common glutamate/P5C manifold.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route is topologically coherent and reaches proline through a plausible P5C closure, but key middle-to-late steps are weak. Hydrothermal formate formation is strong, acetate is acceptable, and pyruvate from CO2-derived feedstocks is plausible but still limited experimentally. The major weaknesses are the speculative homologation to 2-oxoglutarate and especially the direct partial reduction of glutamate to glutamate semialdehyde under prebiotic conditions. Final P5C cyclization is good; reduction to proline is plausible but not strongly established without specific reducing systems.
suggestions: Replace rxn_013 with a better-supported C4-to-C5 extension sequence, ideally via experimentally grounded cyanohydrin or keto-acid chemistry.
Reframe rxn_016 as a mineral/redox-coupled sequence with a defined activating intermediate rather than direct glutamate reduction.
Specify mineral or redox agents for late-stage biochemical reductions to improve plausibility.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The alanine-assisted branch adds realistic amino transfer logic, and alanine from pyruvate is one of the stronger steps. However, the construction of oxaloacetate from pyruvate plus HCN and phosphate is underdefined and chemically weak as written. Downstream conversion to 2-oxoglutarate and glutamate semialdehyde remains the dominant bottleneck. Overall, the route is connected but relies on two speculative transformations in sequence.
suggestions: Provide an explicit cyanohydrin-hydrolysis sequence or alternative route to oxaloacetate.
Use aspartate-derived chemistry with clearer carbon accounting for 2-oxoglutarate formation.
Include mechanistic justification for reductive conversion from glutamate to semialdehyde.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the weakest major route. Hydrolysis of HCN to formamide is plausible, but direct generation of HCN from only CO2 and NH3 in a surface cyanosulfidic context is not credible without additional reduced carbon/nitrogen feedstocks. Conversion of formamide to a formaldehyde reservoir is also poorly grounded. The upper surface network therefore rests on weak entry chemistry, although later aldol and transamination logic is more reasonable. The route reaches proline formally, but with significant speculative burden early and late.
suggestions: Replace rxn_005 with a more established HCN source involving methane/nitriles/impact or atmospheric photochemistry.
Replace rxn_007 with a literature-supported formamide photochemistry product set rather than formaldehyde directly.
Clarify oxidation chemistry in rxn_010 and define plausible mineral photoredox agents.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Starting at glycolaldehyde improves the network by avoiding the weakest HCN-entry chemistry, and the aldol extension to glyceraldehyde is plausible. However, oxidative assembly of pyruvate from glyceraldehyde remains weak, and the same oxaloacetate, 2-oxoglutarate, and glutamate semialdehyde bottlenecks remain. This is a more reasonable surface-centered route than P3, but still not approval-grade.
suggestions: Introduce a better-supported route from triose chemistry to pyruvate or phosphoenol-like intermediates.
Strengthen C4 and C5 assembly steps with more explicit intermediates.
Avoid direct semialdehyde generation from glutamate unless activation chemistry is included.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This mixed-environment route is one of the strongest from a systems perspective because it genuinely converges hydrothermal and surface pyruvate inputs. The problem is that convergence does not solve the weak chemistry of the downstream C4-to-C5 section. Environmental interconnection is very good, but the route still depends on speculative oxaloacetate/2-oxoglutarate/glutamate-semialdehyde transformations.
suggestions: Retain this as a scaffold but rebuild the C4-to-proline segment using better-supported keto-acid and aminonitrile chemistry.
Add explicit transport/concentration logic for hydrothermal products entering surface pools.
Specify a mineral-assisted reduction system for the final two reductions.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The concept of combining hydrothermal formate with surface C4 assembly is attractive and improves network integration. But the route inherits both the weak HCN entry step and the underdefined oxaloacetate construction. As with other routes, the later glutamate-semialdehyde step is a major prebiotic gap. Good network logic, insufficient reaction support.
suggestions: Keep formate as a cross-environment feedstock, but route C4 synthesis through more explicit carboxylation or cyanohydrin hydrolysis chemistry.
Replace or better justify the HCN source.
Provide experimentally anchored semialdehyde formation chemistry.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The pyroglutamate branch is chemically more coherent than the direct semialdehyde branch in its first closure step: glutamate to pyroglutamate by dehydration/cyclization is plausible. However, reduction/interconversion of pyroglutamate to P5C is weak and poorly supported prebiotically. This pathway avoids one problematic step but replaces it with another. It is conceptually useful as an auxiliary branch, not a strong primary route.
suggestions: Treat pyroglutamate as a side sink unless stronger evidence for conversion to P5C is available.
If retained, specify a defined redox/mineral system for pyroglutamate activation and reduction.
Improve the upstream C5 formation to make this branch more defensible.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is among the better routes overall because the hydrothermal front end is relatively conservative, alanine formation is good, and pyroglutamate formation from glutamate is plausible. The main issue is still the speculative C5 homologation and the weak conversion of pyroglutamate to P5C. Better than many alternatives, but still below approval threshold.
suggestions: Prioritize this branch only if pyroglutamate-to-P5C chemistry can be experimentally supported.
Rebuild the C5 assembly step from aspartate/oxaloacetate with explicit intermediates.
Consider P5C generation via semialdehyde from a more activated glutamate derivative instead of pyroglutamate.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Alanine-mediated transfer gives this route sensible amino-group economy, and the mixed-environment progression is strong. Nevertheless, the route still depends on an underdefined oxaloacetate formation, a speculative homologation to 2-oxoglutarate, and an especially weak glutamate-to-semialdehyde reduction. Good network design, insufficient chemical grounding in the key bottleneck zone.
suggestions: Strengthen oxaloacetate synthesis with explicit intermediate chemistry.
Use aminonitrile or hydroxy-acid chemistry to bridge toward glutamate more plausibly.
Define reducing equivalents and catalysts in the final reduction sequence.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: As a network-integration route this is the strongest: it maximizes convergence, uses multiple hubs, and demonstrates the intended Hydrothermal ↔ Surface → Biochemical architecture very well. However, from a chemistry standpoint it accumulates most of the network's weakest steps rather than resolving them. The route is excellent as a systems map but not yet a robust prebiotic synthesis sequence to proline.
suggestions: Use this as the backbone pathway but replace weak entry and C5-forming reactions with experimentally supported alternatives.
Differentiate high-confidence from speculative branches explicitly.
Provide stronger mechanistic support for the pyroglutamate/P5C interconversion.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is strong topologically and strategically but only moderate chemically. It satisfies most graph-level criteria: it has ample molecule and reaction nodes, good connectivity, multiple hubs, multiple convergent points, and clear environmental flow from hydrothermal feedstocks into surface chemistry and onward to biochemical closure. Pyruvate, oxaloacetate, glutamate, and P5C are sensible organizing hubs, and the network does reach the target molecule. The principal weaknesses are concentrated in chemical feasibility rather than topology. Several early hydrothermal steps are plausible, especially CO2 to formate and pyruvate to alanine. Surface aldol chemistry is also acceptable. However, the route from simple surface feedstocks to HCN is weak as written, formamide to formaldehyde is poorly supported, glyceraldehyde to pyruvate is underdefined, pyruvate plus HCN to oxaloacetate is not convincingly specified, and the aspartate/oxaloacetate to 2-oxoglutarate homologation lacks strong prebiotic grounding. The single biggest bottleneck is the direct reduction of glutamate to glutamate semialdehyde; the pyroglutamate branch partially avoids this but introduces its own weak pyroglutamate-to-P5C conversion. Another issue is that the supplied evaluation summary omits explicit mineral agents and literature citations for the hydrothermal and surface reactions, which would lower environmental and literature-alignment confidence under the original synthesis-network requirements. Overall, this is a well-designed conceptual network for proline-family chemistry, but not yet an approval-grade chemically validated synthesis network.
